name: Villarluengo and Guadalope Gorges Loop
name_original: Sierra Carrascosa – circular a Muela Mujer por Villarluengo – gravel
description: This difficult 21.5-mile gravel touring cycling route in Maestrazgo offers challenging climbs and spectacular, rugged scenery near Villarlue
region_intro: Touring cycling around Villarluengo is characterized by its mountainous terrain within the Maestrazgo comarca of Teruel, Aragon. The region features a landscape of rocky outcrops, deep canyons, and river confluences, encircled by mountain ranges such as Sierra de Garrocha and Sierra de la Cañada. Geological formations like the Órganos de Montoro and the Hoces del Guadalope provide dramatic scenery. The area offers quiet roads with minimal traffic and numerous mountain passes, making it suitable for challenging touring cycling. Best touring cycling routes around Villarluengo The most popular touring cycling route is Sierra del Señor – circular from Montoro de Mezquita via Aldehuela, a 32.3 miles (52.0 km) trail that takes 4 hours 36 minutes to complete. This route navigates mountainous terrain with significant elevation changes. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Villarluengo and Guadalope Gorges Loop, a difficult 21.5 miles (34.7 km) path. This route offers views of the dramatic Hoces del Guadalope and surrounding canyons. Local touring cyclists also love the Iglesuela del Cid – Cantavieja loop from Cantavieja, a 45.2 miles (72.8 km) trail leading through varied landscapes, often completed in about 5 hours 1 minute. Touring cycling around Villarluengo is defined by mountainous terrain, quiet roads with mountain passes, and dramatic canyons like the Hoces del Guadalope. The network primarily offers challenging routes for experienced cyclists. The routes in Villarluengo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 4 reviews. More than 110 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Villarluengo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Villarluengo?
A: There are over 20 touring cycling routes available around Villarluengo, offering a variety of experiences within the mountainous Maestrazgo comarca.

Q: What is the typical difficulty level of touring cycling routes in Villarluengo?
A: Touring cycling routes in Villarluengo are generally challenging, characterized by mountainous terrain, significant elevation changes, and quiet roads with numerous mountain passes. All 21 routes listed are classified as difficult, making the area best suited for experienced cyclists.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in the Villarluengo area?
A: Yes, several circular routes are available. For example, you can explore the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e955740237/sierra-del-senor-circular-from-montoro-de-mezquita-via-aldehuela" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra del Señor – circular from Montoro de Mezquita via Aldehuela&lt;/a&gt;, a 32.3-mile (52.0 km) trail, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e957100950/villarluengo-and-guadalope-gorges-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villarluengo and Guadalope Gorges Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which is 21.5 miles (34.7 km) long and offers views of the dramatic Hoces del Guadalope.

Q: What kind of natural attractions can I expect to see along the cycling routes?
A: The region is rich in natural beauty. You can encounter spectacular geological formations like the Órganos de Montoro, dramatic features such as the Hoces del Guadalope and Cañón del río Guadalope, and scenic river sources like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5247947" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Source of the Pitarque River&lt;/a&gt;. Many routes offer breathtaking panoramic views of the surrounding mountain ranges.

Q: Are there any specific waterfalls or gorges accessible by bike?
A: Yes, the area features several impressive gorges and waterfalls. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5248074" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valloré Footbridges&lt;/a&gt; near Montoro de Mezquita offer a spectacular riverside route. You might also encounter the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3793681" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Waterfall in Barranco de la Hoz Mala on the Guadalope River&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5355841" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Estrecho de Crespol Gorge&lt;/a&gt;, providing dramatic scenery along your ride.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or points of interest in the Villarluengo area?
A: Beyond its natural beauty, Villarluengo itself is a charming historic town with cobbled streets and stone houses. You can see the neoclassical Church of Nuestra Señora de la Asunción and a 16th-century Town Hall. The region also features fortified farmhouses like Torre Santa, and the ruins of the Monte Santo convent. For insights into local wildlife, visit the Centro de la Naturaleza y de la Cabra Montesa.

Q: What is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Villarluengo?
A: The mountainous terrain means that spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant temperatures for cycling, avoiding the extreme heat of summer and potential snow or colder conditions in winter. These seasons also showcase the region's natural landscapes at their most vibrant.

Q: Are there opportunities to see wildlife while cycling in Villarluengo?
A: The Maestrazgo region is known for its rich natural heritage. While specific sightings are never guaranteed, the quiet roads and natural landscapes, particularly within the Maestrazgo Cultural Park, provide opportunities to observe local wildlife. The Centro de la Naturaleza y de la Cabra Montesa offers more information on the area's fauna.

Q: What do other cyclists say about their experience in Villarluengo?
A: The touring cycling routes in Villarluengo are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 4 reviews. Cyclists often praise the dramatic scenery, challenging climbs, and the tranquility of the quiet roads.

Q: Are there any routes that pass through charming villages or offer cafe stops?
A: Many routes in the Maestrazgo region connect charming villages, offering opportunities for breaks and refreshments. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40005252" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Iglesuela del Cid – Cantavieja loop from Cantavieja&lt;/a&gt; leads through varied landscapes and connects these historic towns, where you can find local amenities.

Q: Where can I find parking when planning a cycling trip to Villarluengo?
A: Villarluengo and other villages in the Maestrazgo comarca typically offer public parking areas. It's advisable to check local signage upon arrival in your chosen starting point, such as Villarluengo, Montoro de Mezquita, or Cantavieja, for designated parking spots.

Q: Can I rent bikes in Villarluengo?
A: While Villarluengo is a small village, it's best to check with local tourist information centers or search online for bike rental services in larger nearby towns within the Maestrazgo region if you require rentals. It's not a service commonly found directly in Villarluengo itself.
tips: A dramatic location on the steep sided gorge of the Rio Cañada. PLeasant medaval village with some facilities.
Fortified country house on the road to Tronchon. There is an explanatory panel about this type of farmhouse in the area. Easy to see and curious.

Spectacular road through the gorge of the Palomita River. Paved but in poor condition. Almost continuous slopes and slopes of 10-22%, quite hard. Rocks in the middle of the road if there has been recent rain. Good views of a waterfall on the river, birds, wildlife....... Highly highly recommended, but demanding ramps.

faqs.question: What is the estimated duration for cycling this route?
faqs.answer: The estimated cycling time for the Sierra Carrascosa – circular to Muela Mujer via Villarluengo – gravel route is approximately 3 hours. However, given its 'difficult' rating and challenging terrain, riders should allow extra time, especially if stopping to enjoy the scenery or navigating steep sections.

faqs.question: Is this gravel cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as 'difficult' and requires a very good physical condition. It features significant climbs, including sections with 10-22% gradients, and potentially poor road surfaces. It is not recommended for beginners.

faqs.question: Is the Sierra Carrascosa route a loop?
faqs.answer: Yes, as indicated by its name, the Sierra Carrascosa – circular to Muela Mujer via Villarluengo – gravel is a circular route, meaning you will finish at your starting point.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Sierra Carrascosa gravel route?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas are not detailed, you would typically look for parking in or near the town of Villarluengo, which is a key point along the route and a common access point for the Maestrazgo region.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this gravel route?
faqs.answer: The route features mostly paved tracks, but be prepared for sections in poor condition, especially through the Palomita River Gorge, which has almost continuous slopes and climbs up to 22%. The terrain is often described as 'pure rock' and 'super steep' in parts, suggesting that tires of at least 40mm are beneficial for comfort and traction.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle the Sierra Carrascosa gravel route?
faqs.answer: Given the rugged and potentially exposed nature of the terrain, spring and autumn are generally the best times to cycle this route. Summers can be very hot in Aragon, and winters may bring challenging conditions, including snow or ice at higher elevations.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the Sierra Carrascosa gravel route?
faqs.answer: Along the route, you'll pass through the picturesque settlement of Villarluengo. Other notable features include a Fortified farmhouse and the scenic Palomita River Gorge. The broader region also boasts dramatic geological formations like 'El Guerrero Romano'.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other cycling or hiking trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with several other paths. Significant portions of it overlap with the Hoces del Guadalope (34%) and [CIMA TE09] Cuarto Pelado (28%). Smaller sections also coincide with El Raspador, Senda del Maestrazgo, Enlace Ruta 1 - Pitarque - Ruta 9, [CIMA TE14] San Cristóbal, Camino del Estrecho, and Camino de Villarluengo.

faqs.question: Is the Sierra Carrascosa – circular to Muela Mujer via Villarluengo – gravel route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the Maestrazgo region is generally welcoming to outdoor activities, this specific route is designed for touring bicycles. Cycling with a dog, especially on a difficult route with steep gradients and potentially rough surfaces, would be very challenging and is generally not recommended for the safety and comfort of both rider and animal.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee to cycle in the Maestrazgo region?
faqs.answer: No, there are typically no permits or entrance fees required for general cycling on public trails within the Maestrazgo comarca where this route is located. The area is known for its open access to natural landscapes.

faqs.question: What should I pack for this challenging gravel ride?
faqs.answer: Given the route's difficulty and remote nature, it's crucial to pack essentials. This includes sufficient water and snacks, a repair kit for your bike (including spare tubes and a pump), appropriate clothing layers for changing weather, a first-aid kit, and a fully charged phone with offline maps. Consider tires of at least 40mm for better performance on the varied terrain.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or restaurants near the route in Villarluengo?
faqs.answer: Yes, Villarluengo is a small settlement along the route. While options may be limited, you can typically find local cafes or restaurants there to refuel before or after your ride. It's advisable to check opening hours in advance, especially outside of peak season.
